none: Environmental Impact Statement, Central City Urban Renewal Project, Parksburg, West Virginia

The Central City Urban Renewal Project in Parkersburg, West Virginia, aimed to revitalize a blighted downtown area by demolishing substandard buildings and constructing new governmental facilities and a hotel. The project, which began in 1969, faced controversy over the proposed demolition of the historic Wood County Courthouse and Jail, which were eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. The project promised to improve traffic flow, create jobs, and boost the local economy, but faced opposition from historic preservation advocates who argued for retaining the courthouse and jail.

The Central City Urban Renewal Project in Parkersburg, West Virginia, aimed to revitalize a blighted downtown area by demolishing substandard buildings and constructing new governmental facilities and a hotel. The project, which began in 1969, faced controversy over the proposed demolition of the historic Wood County Courthouse and Jail, which were eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. The project promised to improve traffic flow, create jobs, and boost the local economy, but faced opposition from historic preservation advocates who argued for retaining the courthouse and jail.
Department of Housing and Urban Development
West Augusta Historic and Genealogical Society

The Central City Urban Renewal Project was proposed to revitalize a deteriorating downtown area in Parkersburg, West Virginia, starting in 1969. It aimed to replace substandard buildings with new government facilities and a hotel, but faced significant public pushback due to plans to demolish the historic Wood County Courthouse and Jail, which were eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. Ultimately, the project proceeded despite the opposition, reflecting the ongoing tension between urban development and historic preservation.
"The bureaucratic delays involved in this subject are beyond toleration." — William P. A. Nicely, Mayor of Parkersburg

"The rhetoric that we receive is revolting and it is time for some organization to go to bat and make known to the world, if necessary, that HUD and the Department of the Interior are LIP SERVICE organizations appealing to only a few and not to the majority." — George A. Kellenberger, Executive Vice President, Greater Parkersburg Chamber of Commerce
